description: The provided image is a low-resolution graphical user interface button displaying the text "Check for updates", which shows no identifiable correlation with the associated research on low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels. No valid information related to the five studied Al-C steel grades (S1 to S5), their alloy compositions, heat treatment procedures, microstructural characteristics including nano-bainite, retained austenite and carbide precipitates, mechanical performance data, or bainitic transformation regulation rules can be extracted from this image, indicating that the image is likely mismatched, incorrectly uploaded, or corrupted, and cannot support any knowledge fusion for the related material science research.

This figure includes two subfigures for the studied low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels (S1 to S5). Subfigure (a) schematically illustrates the heat treatment cycles: the heating rate to austenitizing temperature T_A is 10 K s⁻¹, with T_A parameters being 1150 ℃ for 15 min for S1, S2, S5, 1220 ℃ for 20 min for S3, and 1150/1250 ℃ for 15 min each for S4. Samples are cooled at 100 K s⁻¹ to sample-specific austempering temperature T_B, all above the martensite start temperature Ms, for isothermal holding. Subfigure (b) presents dilatometric analysis results to determine bainitic transformation finish time Bt: the black curve shows relative length change (RLC, %) versus time, while the red curve shows transformation rate (s⁻¹) versus time. Bt is defined as the onset of the RLC plateau around 1600 s when transformation rate drops to a negligible level, to ensure near-full bainitic transformation during austempering. This figure clarifies the heat treatment protocol and kinetic determination method for the studied steel system.

This figure demonstrates the method for determining retained austenite volume fraction ($V_\gamma$) using S1 low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steel after isothermal bainitic treatment at 350 °C as an example. Subplot (a) shows the X-ray diffraction pattern of the treated S1 sample, with indexed peaks for bcc α (bainitic ferrite: (110)α, (200)α, (211)α, (220)α) and fcc γ (retained austenite: (111)γ, (200)γ, (220)γ, (311)γ); the integrated intensity of the (200)γ peak (marked by the red box, with an amplified inset) is collected for $V_\gamma$ quantification. Subplot (b) presents MatCalc-simulated equilibrium phase fractions of S1 steel as a function of temperature, where bcc is the dominant phase, accompanied by minor phases including κ-carbide, hcp phase, (Fe,M)$_x$C$_y$ carbide and AlN; the value $(1-V_c) = 0.78$ at the 300~350 °C bainitic transformation range is extracted for $V_\gamma$ calculation correction. This figure validates the combined XRD peak integration and thermodynamic simulation approach for retained austenite quantification in the studied high-Al bainitic steel system.

This figure presents dilatometric analysis of austenitizing response and light optical microscopy (LOM) verification of polygonal ferrite (αₚ) for 5 low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels (S1-S5, rows a-e). Left column shows relative length change (RLC) vs temperature heating curves (black) and their first derivatives d(RLC)/dT (red), used to determine operational austenitizing temperature Tₐ (marked by vertical dashed line: 1062 °C for S1, 1031 °C for S2, 1067 °C for S4, ~900 °C for S5; S3 shows broadened austenitizing transition without unique Tₐ). Middle column shows 1050 °C benchmark LOM images (100 μm scale bar), confirming αₚ retention in S1, S3, S4, S5 and αₚ-free microstructure in S2 at this temperature. Right column shows LOM at selected Tₐ (1150 °C for S1/S2/S4/S5, 1220 °C for S3): αₚ is eliminated in S1/S2, reduced to trace levels in S4/S5, while S3 retains noticeable αₚ even at 1220 °C, consistent with high Al's ferrite-stabilizing effect. It is concluded that 1150 °C is optimized austenitizing temperature for S1, S2, S4, S5, while high-Al S3 requires 1220 °C to minimize αₚ.

This group of 5 subplots (S1 to S5) presents MatCalc simulated equilibrium phase fraction versus temperature diagrams for 5 low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels, with temperature (℃) as the x-axis and log-scaled phase fraction as the y-axis. Detected phases include bcc (green curve), fcc (red curve), κ-carbide, hcp phase, (Fe,M)ₓCᵧ carbide, and TiC (blue curve that only appears in Ti-containing S4). The ferrite-free austenitization temperature Tₐ and carbide dissolution temperature Tcd are marked for each steel: S1 (Tₐ=991℃, Tcd=1103℃), S2 (Tₐ=929℃, Tcd=1107℃), S3 (Tₐ=1177℃, Tcd=1103℃), S4 (Tₐ=1016℃, Tcd=1134℃), S5 (Tₐ=986℃, Tcd=775℃). These results reveal the effect of alloy composition (especially Al and Ti content) on phase transition and carbide dissolution behaviors, providing guidance for optimizing austenitization heat treatment parameters for each tested steel.

This set of plots determines the martensite-start temperature (Ms) of five low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5) via cooling dilatometry, where relative length change (RLC, %) is plotted against temperature (°C). Ms is defined as the tangent intersection of the linear contraction segment (dashed lines) on the curves. Measured Ms values are 277 °C for S1, 274 °C for S2, 258 °C for S3, 310 °C for S4 austenitized at 1250 °C, 305 °C for S4 austenitized at 1150 °C (negligible difference within experimental scatter, indicating no obvious effect of Ti microalloying on Ms), and 331 °C for S5. Lower Ms in S1–S3 indicates effective carbon enrichment in austenite after austenitization, with the lowest Ms in S3 attributed to κ-carbide dissolution and retained ferrite facilitating carbon partitioning. Higher Ms in S5 corresponds to lower effective carbon in austenite. The overall Ms trends are consistent with alloy chemistries and austenitization analysis, supporting the heat-treatment strategy that austempering temperatures slightly above Ms promote bainitic transformation while suppressing martensite formation.

This set of figures presents bainitic transformation kinetics of low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels (samples S1-S5 with varying Al, C and alloying element contents) measured via dilatometry, plotted as relative length change (RLC, %) versus time (s). Subfigures (a) and (b) adopt logarithmic time axes: (a) shows data for steels with Al ≥ 6 wt.% (S1, S2, S3) under different isothermal bainitic holding temperatures (270℃, 300℃, 350℃); (b) shows data for steels with Al < 6 wt.% (S4, S5) under different austenitization and isothermal holding temperatures (300℃, 335℃, 350℃, 365℃). Subfigures (c)-(e) replot the same data with linear time axes for better readability of transformation times: (c) for S1/S2, (d) for the slow-transforming S3 with extended time range, (e) for S4/S5, with vertical dashed lines marking approximate bainite finish times. Key trends show higher isothermal temperature accelerates transformation, higher Al content remarkably slows transformation (bainite finish time of S3 reaches up to 43000 s), while Ti-microalloyed S4 and low-Al S5 have much faster transformation (minimum finish time ~700 s). The figure supports that Al content, isothermal temperature and Ti microalloying significantly regulate bainitic transformation kinetics of the tested steels.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of 5 low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels (S1 to S5) treated under different austenitization temperatures (T_A: 1150℃, 1220℃, 1250℃) and austempering temperatures (T_B: 270℃ to 365℃). Four mechanical indicators are plotted: ultimate tensile strength (UTS, black squares, unit MPa), elongation (El, red triangles, unit %), 0.2% offset yield strength (Rp0.2, blue downward triangles, unit MPa), and Vickers hardness (HV30, black circles, with values labeled below each sample group). Key findings include that Ti-microalloyed S4 steel obtains the highest El at T_A=1150℃, T_B=350℃, with average 10% and maximum 12%, outperforming S1 and S2. Hardness of all samples ranges from 443 HV30 to 500 HV30, UTS ranges from ~1300 MPa to 1600 MPa, Rp0.2 rises obviously with decreasing T_B, while El generally increases with rising T_B. This figure supports the conclusion that combined alloy design and heat treatment optimization can achieve excellent strength-ductility balance of nano-bainitic steels, and lower alloying concept suppresses bainite lath thickness heterogeneity to improve elongation.

This figure shows four MatCalc-simulated Time-Temperature-Precipitation (TTP) diagrams for investigated low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels: subplot (a) for steel S1 (approximately representative for S2), (b) for S3, (c) for S4, (d) for S5. All diagrams use log-scaled time (s) as x-axis and temperature (°C) as y-axis. Curves in each diagram mark 5%, 50%, 95% precipitation fractions of (Fe,M)ₓCᵧ carbides, and subplot (c) additionally marks the precipitation region of TiMoC complex carbides. Each subplot labels the austenitization temperature Tₐ, a uniform cooling rate of 100 K s⁻¹, and austempering temperature Tᵦ with corresponding holding time Bt. Comparisons show that (Fe,M)ₓCᵧ precipitation kinetics is extremely fast between ~700 °C and 1150 °C in S1/S3, and 700-1000 °C in S5, making their avoidance impossible. For S4, initial (Fe,M)ₓCᵧ nucleation between 700-950 °C is followed by suppression from the counter-coarsening effect of TiMoC precipitates. S3 shows slightly slower (Fe,M)ₓCᵧ precipitation kinetics than S1, while S5 has a narrower precipitation temperature range. These TTP diagrams reveal the regulation effect of alloying elements (Al, Ti, Mo) on precipitation kinetics, supporting optimization of heat treatment processes for the studied steels.

This figure displays microstructural characterization results of a series of low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels with varied C, Al, and microalloying (Ti/Ni) contents, after different austenitization and isothermal bainitic transformation heat treatments. Most subfigures are high-magnification scanning electron microscopy images, with partial subfigures marked with 1 μm scale bar, showing lamellar nano-bainitic structures, where arrows explicitly mark the precipitated (Fe,M)xCy carbide phase. Two lower-magnification metallographic images with 50 μm scale bar reveal overall phase distribution including bainitic sheaves, retained austenite, and proeutectoid ferrite in certain samples. Morphology differences including bainite lamella thickness, carbide distribution, and phase orientation are observed across different samples, verifying that alloy composition and heat treatment parameters regulate the microstructure of the nano-bainitic steel system, supporting the correlation between microstructure and mechanical performance of the developed steels.

This figure presents SEM micrographs and EDS elemental mapping analysis of Ti-alloyed low-density nano-bainitic S4 Al-C steel under varied heat treatment conditions. Subfigures (a), (b), (c) are SEM images: (a) corresponds to the sample austenitized at 1250 °C and isothermally quenched at 350 °C, (b) corresponds to the sample austenitized at 1150 °C and isothermally quenched at 350 °C, (c) corresponds to the sample austenitized at 1150 °C and isothermally quenched at 320 °C. All SEM images exhibit lath-shaped bainitic matrix with bright TiMoC precipitates distributed, while AlN phase is also identified in partial samples. Subfigure (d) is the EDS mapping of the region in (c) with a 10 μm scale, showing element distributions of Fe, C, Ti and Mo, confirming that the precipitates are Ti-Mo-C-rich carbides depleted of Fe, verifying the formation of TiMoC complex precipitates. These results support that TiMoC precipitates form during post-casting cooling, act as bainite nucleation sites, and suppress formation of undesired (Fe,M)xCy, MoC carbides.

This figure presents microstructure characterization of S5 low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steel austempered at two temperatures (335 °C and 365 °C). Subfigures (a) and (b) are SEM images: (a) for 335 °C sample shows bainitic lamellae inside lenticular martensite; (b) for 365 °C sample exhibits AlN precipitates, grain boundary αp, and impingement-free film-like retained austenite (γr). Subfigure (c) is XRD patterns of both samples, showing only α (110) and γ (111) peaks with no detectable martensite peaks, confirming martensite fraction is below XRD detection limit. Subfigures (d)-(f) are EBSD inverse pole figure maps: (d)(e) for 335 °C sample present fine bainite with scattered martensite (M); (f) for 365 °C sample shows slightly coarser bainite with trace ferrite (F). Subfigures (g)-(i) are kernel average misorientation maps: average KAM is 0.825° for 335 °C sample with local spikes near M, and 0.905° for 365 °C sample with marginally higher global KAM. This figure confirms austempering temperature tunes the microstructure of S5 steel: lower TB yields finer bainite and minor martensite with higher strength but lower ductility, while higher TB leads to coarser bainite and higher γr fraction with improved ductility, supporting the heat treatment-microstructure-property correlation of the steel.
